trade.summary: Germany is Switzerland's largest trading partner. Deep economic integration through proximity, language, and bilateral agreements. Switzerland exports pharmaceuticals, chemicals, machinery, and financial services. Germany exports vehicles, machinery, and chemicals.

diplomatic.summary: Exceptionally close relations built on shared language, culture, and values. Switzerland is not an EU member but participates in Schengen and has bilateral treaties with the EU (including Germany). Coordination on financial regulation, cross-border labor, and tax policy is ongoing.

military.summary: Switzerland's neutrality precludes NATO membership, but informal security dialogue exists. Switzerland purchases defense equipment from Germany and participates in some European security forums. No joint exercises as formal partners.

financial.summary: Deeply intertwined financial systems. Major German companies maintain Swiss headquarters or subsidiaries. Swiss banks operate extensively in Germany. Bilateral investment stock is among the highest for either country.

energy.summary: Switzerland exports hydroelectric and nuclear power to Germany; Germany exports natural gas to Switzerland. Cross-border electricity trade is significant. Both countries cooperating on European energy transition.

scientific.summary: Intensive scientific collaboration. ETH Zurich and German universities maintain extensive joint programs. Shared language facilitates deep academic ties. Co-publications in pharma, engineering, and physics are among the most productive bilateral pairs in Europe.
